header: 1. PURPOSE AND BENEFITS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS
context: This section discusses the advantages and reasons for investing in investment-linked sub-funds, such as diversification, professional management, and flexibility.
text:
<context>This section discusses the advantages and reasons for investing in investment-linked sub-funds, such as diversification, professional management, and flexibility.</context>
# 1. PURPOSE AND BENEFITS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS

1.1 The benefits of investing in investment-linked sub-funds include:

diversification of investment portfolio; different investment fund options;   
professional expertise;   
ease of transactions;   
liquidity; and   
flexibility

context: This chunk introduces the two main structures of investment-linked sub-funds: Accumulation and Distribution, and mentions their applicability to unit trusts.
text:
<context>This chunk introduces the two main structures of investment-linked sub-funds: Accumulation and Distribution, and mentions their applicability to unit trusts.</context>
# 2. STRUCTURES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS

2.1 There are two possible structures of investment-linked sub-funds, namely Accumulation Structures and Distribution Structures. The comments that follow in this chapter also apply to unit trusts which are another form of collective investment schemes.

context: This chunk describes "Accumulation Structures" as one of two possible structures for investment-linked sub-funds, detailing how investment income is reinvested within the fund to enhance unit prices. This follows a section on the general structures of such funds.
text:
<context>This chunk describes "Accumulation Structures" as one of two possible structures for investment-linked sub-funds, detailing how investment income is reinvested within the fund to enhance unit prices. This follows a section on the general structures of such funds.</context>
# A. Accumulation Structures

The investment income of these funds is ploughed (put) back into the fund.   
Hence, the unit prices will be enhanced.

context: This chunk discusses the characteristics of "Distribution Structures" for investment-linked sub-funds, contrasting them with "Accumulation Structures" and explaining how income is distributed to policy owners. It appears in Chapter 2, which details the different structures of these funds.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the characteristics of "Distribution Structures" for investment-linked sub-funds, contrasting them with "Accumulation Structures" and explaining how income is distributed to policy owners. It appears in Chapter 2, which details the different structures of these funds.</context>
# B. Distribution Structures

2.3 The investment income of these funds is distributed to the policy owners. The unit prices will still vary as a result of capital gains or losses. However, the policy owners will regularly receive income in the form of additional units or dividend payments.

2.4 The full benefits and risks of ILPs are passed on to the policy owners, regardless of whether the units are applied, based on the accumulation or distribution structures.

context: This chunk introduces the definitions of various terms related to investment-linked sub-funds, which will be detailed in the following sections.
text:
<context>This chunk introduces the definitions of various terms related to investment-linked sub-funds, which will be detailed in the following sections.</context>
# DEFINITIONS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS

3.1 Here are some key definitions that are sometimes used to describe the nature of a fund or a relationship between funds.

context: This chunk defines "Mother Fund" as a type of investment-linked sub-fund, appearing in Chapter 3 which covers definitions.
text:
<context>This chunk defines "Mother Fund" as a type of investment-linked sub-fund, appearing in Chapter 3 which covers definitions.</context>
# A. Mother Fund

3.2

A mother fund is an existing fund that was incepted at an earlier point in time.   
It may eventually be tapped into by subsequent funds.

context: This chunk defines a "Feeder Fund" in relation to a "Mother Fund," as part of the broader "DEFINITIONS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section.
text:
<context>This chunk defines a "Feeder Fund" in relation to a "Mother Fund," as part of the broader "DEFINITIONS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section.</context>
# B. Feeder Fund

3.3 A feeder fund is a fund that feeds into an existing fund (i.e. the mother fund). Although the size of the feeder fund may actually differ from that of the mother fund, it taps directly into the mother fund.

3.4 As a result of this, the feeder fund experiences the same level of fluctuation in terms of unit price movements.

context: This chunk defines a "Mirror Fund" in the "Definitions of Investment-Linked Sub-Funds" section, explaining how it replicates an existing fund's composition.
text:
<context>This chunk defines a "Mirror Fund" in the "Definitions of Investment-Linked Sub-Funds" section, explaining how it replicates an existing fund's composition.</context>
# C. Mirror Fund

3.5 Some funds are designed according to an existing fund. A mirror fund follows closely the composition of an existing fund. The mirror fund will attempt to duplicate the composition of the existing fund in terms of asset, geographical and sector allocation, as well as in terms of investment selection.

context: This chunk defines "Portfolios" as a preset mix of funds offered by financial institutions, and it is located within the "DEFINITIONS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section of the document.
text:
<context>This chunk defines "Portfolios" as a preset mix of funds offered by financial institutions, and it is located within the "DEFINITIONS OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section of the document.</context>
# D. Portfolios

3.6 In order to help investors overcome the complexities of investment selection, insurers and financial institutions sometimes offer a preset mix of funds to investors (based on the investor’s risk profile). This preset mix of funds is referred to as a portfolio.

context: This chunk introduces the section on the various types of investment-linked sub-funds, following the discussion of their purpose, benefits, structures, and definitions.
text:
<context>This chunk introduces the section on the various types of investment-linked sub-funds, following the discussion of their purpose, benefits, structures, and definitions.</context>
# 4. TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS

4.1 Investment-linked sub-funds can be invested in most financial instruments up to the maximum as allowed by the MAS. Broadly, the types of funds are as follows:

context: This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing the characteristics of Equity Funds.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing the characteristics of Equity Funds.</context>
# A. Equity Funds

These are funds that concentrate their investments in equity assets, which generally aim for capital appreciation.

header: B. Fixed Income Or Bond Funds
context: This chunk discusses the characteristics and risks of Fixed Income or Bond Funds, which is one of the types of investment-linked sub-funds detailed in Section 4.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the characteristics and risks of Fixed Income or Bond Funds, which is one of the types of investment-linked sub-funds detailed in Section 4.</context>
# B. Fixed Income Or Bond Funds

4.3 These funds invest in corporate bonds, government securities and other forms of fixed income instruments. The assets of these funds are chosen on the basis of their income producing characteristics.

Most risks of fixed income funds include interest rate risk, credit risk and reinvestment risk.

context: This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically under subsection C, "Cash Funds." It describes these funds as also being known as Money Market Funds and explains their investment focus and low volatility.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically under subsection C, "Cash Funds." It describes these funds as also being known as Money Market Funds and explains their investment focus and low volatility.</context>
# C. Cash Funds

4.5 These funds are also known as Money Market Funds. They invest only in cash and its equivalents. As a result, the capital values of these funds are unlikely to have high volatility.

context: This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically under subsection D, "Managed Funds." It describes the composition and characteristics of managed funds, also known as balanced funds.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically under subsection D, "Managed Funds." It describes the composition and characteristics of managed funds, also known as balanced funds.</context>
# D. Managed Funds

4.6 These tend to have a "managed" basket of assets, usually comprising a higher proportion of equities and a lower proportion of fixed income instruments. Sometimes, property or other assets can be part of the portfolio. They are also known as Balanced Funds, when there is an apportionment of investments between equity and fixed income instruments, within a pre-determined range.

context: This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing "Property Funds" as a type of investment-linked sub-fund, highlighting their investment in real estate and associated liquidity challenges.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing "Property Funds" as a type of investment-linked sub-fund, highlighting their investment in real estate and associated liquidity challenges.</context>
# E. Property Funds

4.7 These funds invest in properties and property shares. As property is an illiquid asset, it is not always possible to liquidate the assets quickly, when policy owners want to sell their units. Hence, property funds normally include a provision under which unit redemptions, except on death, can be deferred typically up to 12 months by the fund manager.

context: Section 4. Types of Investment-Linked Sub-Funds, under subsection F. Geographically Specialised Unit Funds, describes funds that focus investments on specific countries or regions.
text:
<context>Section 4. Types of Investment-Linked Sub-Funds, under subsection F. Geographically Specialised Unit Funds, describes funds that focus investments on specific countries or regions.</context>
# F. Geographically Specialised Unit Funds

4.8 Some funds diversify investments over the world or restrict investments to a particular country or region, for example:

ASEAN Growth; Japan; and European.

context: This chunk is part of a larger section (Section 4) that categorizes different types of investment-linked sub-funds. Specifically, it details "Specialised Unit Funds," also known as "Sector Funds," which focus on particular industries or sectors.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of a larger section (Section 4) that categorizes different types of investment-linked sub-funds. Specifically, it details "Specialised Unit Funds," also known as "Sector Funds," which focus on particular industries or sectors.</context>
# G. Specialised Unit Funds

4.9 Other funds restrict investments to particular industries or sectors, for example:

gold;   
finance;   
technology;   
commodities; and small capitalisation companies.

4.10 These funds are also known as Sector Funds.

context: This chunk is a part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing "Capital Guaranteed Funds" (section 4.11 and 4.12).
text:
<context>This chunk is a part of section 4, "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS," specifically detailing "Capital Guaranteed Funds" (section 4.11 and 4.12).</context>
# H. Capital Guaranteed Funds

4.11 With Capital Guaranteed Funds, policy owners are offered a combination of security, as well as investment opportunity. Capital Guaranteed Funds are funds that promise to return a minimum amount, usually after a certain period of time, or at some particular points of time.

4.12 In most structures, a significant amount of the funds collected is invested into fixed income instruments (usually bonds), in order to preserve a portion of the capital of the policy owners. In order to give potential investment growth to the funds, the balance of the funds is then used to purchase derivatives (usually options, which are standardised contracts giving the buyer the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell an asset at a specified time and at a pre-set price). Capital Guaranteed Funds are commonly closed-end funds meaning that they have a limited subscription period, as well as a fixed maturity date. They are commonly issued with a tenure (holding period) of around four to seven years.

context: This chunk, detailing Managed Portfolios and presenting Table 8.1 on common fund types and their risk characteristics, is part of the "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section (Section 4), specifically following the discussion on Property Funds and preceding Capital Guaranteed Funds.
text:
<context>This chunk, detailing Managed Portfolios and presenting Table 8.1 on common fund types and their risk characteristics, is part of the "TYPES OF INVESTMENT-LINKED SUB-FUNDS" section (Section 4), specifically following the discussion on Property Funds and preceding Capital Guaranteed Funds.</context>
# I. Managed Portfolios

4.13 Managed Portfolios (also known as Risk Rated or Lifestyle Funds) come with a pre-set mix of funds. Depending on the objective of the portfolios, the investment manager will then decide on the amount of investment to be put into an Equity Fund and / or Fixed Income Fund.

4.14 This should not be confused with a Managed Fund, where there is only one fund and one fund manager. In a Managed Fund, the fund manager decides on which asset to invest in. With Managed Portfolios, there are potentially multiple funds and one investment manager. Here, the investment manager decides on which fund or funds to invest in.

4.15 Table 8.1 shows some of the more common types of funds available along with an indication of their risk characteristics. The risk classification can only be a very broad guide, since the risks associated with any fund will depend upon how, precisely, it is invested. For example, a Bond Fund specialising in low investment grade bonds will be a high-risk investment. More information on the risk classification of funds authorised to participate in the CPF Investment Scheme is available on the CPF website.

Table 8.1: Some Common Types Of Funds And Their Risk Characteristics

narrative: For Managed Portfolios, Equity Funds are primarily invested in company stocks with the general aim of capital appreciation, fall into a Medium to High risk category, and have a minimum time horizon of Ten years plus.

For Managed Portfolios, Income, Fixed Interest And Bond Funds are invested in corporate bonds, government securities and other fixed income securities, fall into a Medium risk category, and have a minimum time horizon of Four years plus.

For Managed Portfolios, Cash Funds, sometimes known as Money Market Funds, are invested in cash, bank deposits and other money market instruments, fall into a Low risk category, and have a minimum time horizon of Up to three years.
For Managed Portfolios, Balanced Funds combine equity investment with fixed interest instruments, fall into a Medium risk category, and have a minimum time horizon of Four years plus.

For Managed Portfolios, Geographically Specialised Funds have investments restricted to a particular country or region, fall into a High risk category, and have a minimum time horizon of Ten years plus.

# 5. INVESTMENT STRATEGY OR POLICY

5.1 Each fund will pursue a publicly stated investment strategy or policy. This strategy is normally prominently stated in the sales promotion literature. Some samples described investment strategies are as follows:

# A. Managed Fund

# B. Equity Fund

5.3

# C. Bond Fund

context: This chunk discusses the "Switching Facility" for investment-linked sub-funds, explaining what it is, how it works, and its benefits, before briefly touching on how policy owners can monitor fund performance.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the "Switching Facility" for investment-linked sub-funds, explaining what it is, how it works, and its benefits, before briefly touching on how policy owners can monitor fund performance.</context>
# 6. SWITCHING FACILITY

6.1 If the insurer offers more than one fund to its policy owners, it will normally include a switching facility. The switching facility allows a policy owner freedom to move part or all his money from one fund to another. Such switching usually does not incur any cost, and the units are transacted at bid-to-bid price. When selecting or switching an investment-linked sub-fund, it is important that a policy owner takes his risk profile, investment objective and time horizon into consideration.

6.2 Normally, a policy owner will be entitled to a limited number of switches in a year, without incurring charges.

6.3 The switching facility can be very useful in retirement and child education planning. As the date on which money is required approaches, it is advisable for policy owners to switch assets in the equity type funds (which are more variable in returns) to Cash or Fixed Income Funds, where the capital values are more stable. (Do note that the capital value of a Fixed Income Fund is still subject to interest rate risk, credit risk and reinvestment risk.)

6.4 Do not confuse fund switching with improper product switching, where the client is advised to surrender one product and buy another, and such a switch produces no real benefit to the client. Errant advisers do this to churn extra commissions. Representatives of financial advisers are prohibited to engage in such misconduct for their own selfish interests.

7.1 A policy owner can monitor the investment performance of investment-linked sub-funds by checking the unit prices regularly. Unit prices of investment-linked sub-funds are published in a number of publications, including The Straits Times, The Business Times and Lianhe Zaobao. Some insurers also publish unit prices on their websites.

# Chapter summary:

- Definition or Explanation: Accumulation Structuresof Investment-linked Sub-
The investment income of these funds is ploughed (put) back into the fund. Hence, the unit prices will be enhanced.

- Definition or Explanation: Distribution Structures of Investment-linked Sub-
The investment income of these funds is distributed to the policy owners. The unit prices will still vary as a result of capital gains or losses. However, the policy owners will regularly receive income in the form of additional units or dividend payments.

- Definition or Explanation: Mother Fund
A mother fund is an existing fund that was incepted at an earlier point in time. Itmay eventually be tapped into by subsequent funds.

- Definition or Explanation: Feeder Fund
A feeder fund is a fund that feeds into an existing fund (i.e. the mother fund). Although the size of the feeder fund may actually differ from that of the mother fund, it taps directly into the mother fund. As a result of this, the feeder fund experiences the same level of fluctuation in terms of unit price movements.

- Definition or Explanation: Mirror Fund
A mirror fund follows closely the composition of an existing fund. The mirror fund will attempt to duplicate the composition of the existing fund in terms of asset, geographical and sector allocation, as well as in terms of investment selection.

- Definition or Explanation: Portfolios
To help investors overcome the complexities of investment selection, insurers and financial institutions sometimes offer a preset mix of funds to investors (based on the investor's risk profile). This preset mix of funds is referred to as a portfolio.

- Definition or Explanation: Equity Funds
These are funds that concentrate their investments in equity assets, which generally aim for capital appreciation.

- Definition or Explanation: Fixed Income Or Bond
These funds invest in corporate bonds,government securities and other forms of fixed income instruments. The assets of these funds are chosen on the basis of their income producing characteristics. Most risks of fixed income funds include interest rate risk, credit risk and reinvestment risk.
